what_it_does: A ~1.5MB machine-generated database-catalog snapshot produced by pgdelta (a Supabase migration-diff tool) and parked in a .temp directory. It serializes the full state of a local Postgres database after applying migrations: every schema, table, enum, extension, procedure, index, trigger, role, policy, and dependency. The filename encodes 'local-migrations', a content hash, and a timestamp, so it is a point-in-time catalog used to compute schema diffs.
observations: Top-level keys cover the entire Postgres object space: aggregates, collations, compositeTypes, domains, enums, extensions, procedures, indexes, materializedViews, subscriptions, publications, rlsPolicies, roles, schemas, sequences, tables, triggers, eventTriggers, rules, ranges, views, foreignDataWrappers, servers, userMappings, foreignTables, depends.
Counts: 49 tables, 142 indexes, 44 procedures, 12 enums, 12 schemas, 16 roles, 6 extensions, 5 triggers, 6 eventTriggers, 1 publication, 3 compositeTypes, and 2412 dependency edges.
Object names are namespaced by qualified key (e.g. 'table:public.connector_audit', 'schema:realtime').

Overwhelmingly Supabase platform internals: auth (124 qualified objects), storage (54), realtime/_realtime (56), supabase_functions (8), extensions (6), plus pgbouncer, graphql_public, supabase_migrations.
The only object in the application's own 'public' schema is a single table: public.connector_audit.
Extensions present: uuid-ossp, pg_net, pg_stat_statements, pgcrypto, plpgsql, supabase_vault.
Postgres server version recorded as 170006; currentUser is 'postgres'.
Each column/object carries fine-grained metadata (row_security, replica_identity, custom-type provenance, identity/generated flags, collation, defaults, comments).
standouts: public.connector_audit is the sole app-defined table in an otherwise all-platform catalog — the application footprint here is a single audit table.
2412 dependency edges captured, showing the tool models the full object dependency graph, not just object definitions.
conventions: Follows pgdelta's catalog serialization convention: typed string keys ('<objtype>:<schema>.<name>'), exhaustive per-object metadata mirroring pg_catalog columns.
Lives under a .temp/pgdelta path with a hashed+timestamped filename — a generated build/diff artifact convention, not a hand-authored source file.
concerns: A 1.5MB generated temp artifact is checked into the repo tree under build-prep; .temp directories are normally transient and not version-controlled.
Hash+timestamp filename means it is a frozen snapshot that will go stale relative to live schema and cannot self-update.
notes: Generated pgdelta catalog snapshot of a local Supabase DB — almost entirely stock platform schema, with the only app table being public.connector_audit.
questions: Is this temp snapshot intentionally tracked, or did it leak into the repo from a migration-diff run?
Does public.connector_audit being the lone app table reflect the true intended schema, or was this catalog taken before app migrations were applied?
Which migration set does the filename's content hash correspond to?
purpose_vs_actual: n/a (no stated purpose in-file; by filename it is a pgdelta local-migrations catalog, and its contents match that — a full DB catalog snapshot).
intention_signals: Produced to give pgdelta a complete, comparable picture of the database after local migrations so it can compute the delta against another catalog state and emit migration SQL.
contribution: Contributes a concrete, timestamped baseline of the Supabase database's full catalog state — including the app's connector_audit table and the exact platform extension/role/schema inventory — that records what the local schema looked like at this migration point.
summary_for_embedding: A pgdelta-generated 1.5MB JSON catalog snapshot of a local Supabase Postgres database (49 tables, 142 indexes, 44 procedures, 12 schemas, mostly platform internals with one app table public.connector_audit) used for migration diffing.
intention_for_embedding: To capture the complete post-migration database catalog state as a comparable baseline for computing schema diffs and generating migrations.
